final_answer:
Yes, you can match BSC’s validator system, but only by replacing your current validator-admission path with a native staking-and-election path end to end.

Why it matters: if validator entry/election is not aligned at protocol level, Gold Chain will keep behaving like a custom hybrid, not BSC-style PoSA, which affects decentralization, operations, and security assumptions.

What to do next (production-ready plan, no code changes):
1. Freeze a parity spec first: validator creation flow, key model, minimum self-stake, candidate pool size, active validator count, epoch cadence, delegation rules, rewards, slashing, jail/unjail, and governance controls.
2. Move validator onboarding to native chain staking only; do not depend on bridge/event approval for validator membership.
3. Implement deterministic ranking + election pipeline (candidate set -> active set per epoch) with exact tie-break rules and reproducible outputs.
4. Add full governance parameterization for all staking/election/slashing knobs, with timelock and emergency controls.
5. Align validator key operations with production standards (separate keys, secure signing, sentry topology, key rotation and recovery).
6. Build migration plan for existing validators and stake state so cutover is one-way and clean.
7. Run parity testing against BSC behavior: unit, state-transition replay, fuzzing, byzantine/liveness, long-run soak, and failure recovery drills.
8. Roll out in phases: devnet -> closed testnet -> public testnet -> canary mainnet, with explicit go/no-go gates at each phase.

If you want, next I can give you a strict parity checklist template (control by control) that your team can mark pass/fail before implementation.
